title: Interpreting CO<sub>2</sub> monitoring for assessing airborne transmission risk in indoor environments.
author_string: Jia, Wei, Cheng, Pan, Fontana, Luca, Chan, Pak Wai, Wargocki, Pawel et al.
abstract: Carbon dioxide (CO<sub>2</sub>) monitoring has been used for over a century to indicate indoor air quality, and in recent decades to measure ventilation. However, its use for indicating airborne infection risk remains controversial. CO<sub>2</sub> is emitted by everyone and removed almost solely by ventilation, whereas virus-containing aerosols are emitted only by infectors and removed by other additional mechanisms. Accordingly, methods for interpreting CO<sub>2</sub> monitoring data should be developed, and suitable conditions for its use need to be clarified. We propose two CO<sub>2</sub>-based surrogates, derived from monitored CO<sub>2</sub> concentrations, to better interpret airborne infection risk. The first, per-person dilution (pCO<sub>2</sub>), mirrors CO<sub>2</sub>'s emission rate but adopts the removal dynamics of infectious aerosols. The second, room dilution (rCO<sub>2</sub>), shares pCO<sub>2</sub>'s removal rate but is emitted solely by infected individuals. We define scaling coefficients to estimate these surrogates and compare their temporal profiles with CO<sub>2</sub> concentration excess to assess the impacts of occupant density, ventilation, and non-ventilation removal mechanisms. Using a year-long dataset from two indoor environments, we demonstrate how pCO<sub>2</sub> and rCO<sub>2</sub> effectively indicate steady-state and transient dilution ability, respectively. These surrogates provide a framework for interpreting CO<sub>2</sub> data to assess airborne infection risk under specific conditions.
